title: The First Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
February 18, 2024

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the First Sunday in Lent, beginning the 40-day season of preparation leading to Easter (April 31, 2024). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would be Genesis 2:15-17, 3:1-7 (the Fall of Adam and Eve), Psalm 32, Romans 5:12-19 (Paul on sin and grace), and Matthew 4:1-11 (Jesus's temptation in the wilderness). The sermon occurs during the central penitential season of the Christian year, traditionally marked by fasting, prayer, and self-examination in preparation for Holy Week.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

In early 2024, the Episcopal Church was navigating the aftermath of the 81st General Convention (2022) decisions on LGBTQ+ inclusion and blessing of same-sex relationships, with many dioceses implementing these resolutions into local practice. The Diocese of Colorado, which includes Saint John's Cathedral, was in its normal ecclesiastical calendar with no major bishop elections or diocesan controversies immediately surrounding this date. The broader Anglican Communion continued discussions on biblical interpretation and sexuality that had been ongoing since the 2023 Lambeth Conference, though these were not crisis-level at this moment.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in mid-winter with typical February weather patterns—temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit with intermittent snow possible. City council and mayoral offices under Mayor Mike Johnston were focused on homelessness, public safety, and budget allocations for the upcoming fiscal year. The Capitol Hill neighborhood where Saint John's Cathedral is located was experiencing the gentrification patterns and arts community vitality that characterize the area, though no specific major local story dominated the week of February 11-18, 2024.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The 2024 presidential election was in full swing with Iowa caucuses completed and New Hampshire primary underway; Donald Trump was dominating Republican primary coverage while Joe Biden secured Democratic delegate support. The Senate was debating a bipartisan border security and immigration reform bill that ultimately failed due to Republican opposition. A significant winter storm was moving across parts of the Midwest and Northeast. Ongoing coverage of the war in Ukraine and debates over U.S. military aid continued to dominate foreign policy discourse.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia's invasion of Ukraine marked nearly two years of active conflict, with diplomatic negotiations stalled and military operations ongoing in eastern Ukraine. The Middle East remained tense following escalations between Israel and Hamas in Gaza, with humanitarian concerns dominating international headlines. China's economy and geopolitical tensions with Taiwan remained underlying concerns in global news coverage, though no acute crisis was developing at this specific moment.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in mid-February 2024 existed in an election-saturated environment with significant political polarization visible across all media. The mood was generally anxious about both domestic political division and international conflicts. The Super Bowl (February 11, 2024) had just concluded with Kansas City Chiefs' victory, and winter fatigue was setting in across the northern United States. Popular culture and entertainment provided typical diversions, though the broader national mood reflected concern about economic uncertainty and political direction heading into the general election year.
